title: Part I
paragraphs: These are the papers of Lieutenant Thomas Glahn, which were found among his effects after his death.

It is now two years since I came back from Nordland. When I think of those days my heart warms strangely, and I am filled with a gentle melancholy. I lived in a little hut in the forest, alone, but not lonely. I had my dog Aesop, and the forest, and the sea.

I would wake early in the mornings—before the sun, almost—and listen. The forest was alive with birds. A woodpecker would be hammering somewhere in the pines, and there was always the sound of water, a brook not far from my hut. I would lie and listen for a while, then get up and go out.

The mornings were the best. The world was new each morning. Dew on the grass, mist over the sea below, the air sharp and clean with the smell of pine. I would stand outside my hut and breathe it in and feel something move in my chest—something I could not name.

I had come north to hunt. I was on leave from my regiment, and I had wanted to get away from the town, from the noise and the people. I had found this hut by accident—it belonged to a trading post, and the trader, a man called Mack, had let it to me cheaply. He was a powerful man in those parts, and he had a daughter.
Her name was Edvarda.
But I am getting ahead of myself. I will begin at the beginning.

The first week I was there I saw no one. I hunted in the forest every day, and in the evenings I sat outside my hut and looked at the sea. Aesop would lie beside me, his head on his paws, and sometimes he would raise his head and sniff the air.
It was on the eighth day that I first saw Edvarda.
I was coming back from hunting, Aesop at my heels, when I saw a figure at the edge of the forest. It was a girl—young, with dark hair and quick eyes. She was watching me.
I stopped.
'Good day,' I said.
She did not answer immediately. She looked at me, then at Aesop.
'Is that your dog?' she said.
'Yes,' I said. 'His name is Aesop.'
She came forward and bent to stroke him, and Aesop, who was generally wary of strangers, suffered her touch without protest.
'What have you been hunting?' she asked, straightening up.
'Grouse,' I said. 'And I found some.'
She looked at the birds hanging from my belt.
'You are the man who is staying in our hut,' she said.
'Your hut? Then you are Mack's daughter?'
'Yes. My name is Edvarda. Edvarda Mack.'

She had large eyes, slightly tilted, and she held her head in a particular way, tilted back a little, so that she seemed to be looking at you and past you at the same time. I noticed her hands—they were not quite like other people's hands, the fingers a little too long, the movements too quick and precise.
'I am Glahn,' I said. 'Thomas Glahn. Lieutenant.'
'I know,' she said. 'Father told me. He says you know how to shoot.'
'I manage,' I said.
She looked at me a moment longer, then turned and went back into the forest. I stood watching until she was out of sight.
That evening, sitting outside my hut, I thought about her. The forest was very still. A fish jumped in the sea below. Aesop pricked his ears at the sound, then relaxed again.
I thought about Edvarda Mack.

title: Part II
paragraphs: In the days that followed I saw Edvarda often. She would come to the edge of the forest where I was hunting, or I would go down to the trading post to buy provisions and find her there. We fell into a kind of friendship, though it was not quite friendship—it was something more unsettled than that.

She was unpredictable. Some days she was warm and easy to talk to, and we would sit together on the rocks and watch the sea, and she would ask me questions about my life and the regiment and the south. Other days she was cold and distant, and would hardly speak to me, and look past me as though I were not there.

I did not know what to make of her. She was unlike any woman I had known. She read books and she thought about what she read. Sometimes she would say something that surprised me—some observation about the forest or the sea or about people that was so precise and unexpected that I would stop and look at her.
One afternoon we were sitting on the rocks above the sea. The light was long and golden. She was looking out at the water.
'Do you believe in Pan?' she said suddenly.
'Pan?' I said.
'The old god. Of forests and wild things.'
I thought about this.
'I don't know,' I said. 'Sometimes in the forest I feel something—some presence. I don't know what it is.'
'That is Pan,' she said, with certainty.
I looked at her.
'Do you believe in him?' I asked.
She was silent for a moment.
'I believe in the forest,' she said at last. 'I believe in this.' She gestured at the sea, the rocks, the sky. 'I don't need to give it a name.'
We sat in silence for a while. The sun was going down, and the sea was turning red.
'I will be leaving soon,' she said.
'Leaving?'
'For the winter. I go to the city. Father thinks I should have some education.'
I felt something drop in me at this news, though I had no right to feel anything.
'When?' I asked.
'In a month. Perhaps less.' She turned and looked at me. 'Will you still be here?'
'I don't know,' I said. 'Perhaps.'
She looked at me for a moment, then looked away again.
A month passed. I stayed. I told myself it was because the hunting was good, because I liked the forest and the solitude. I told myself this and partly believed it.
But in truth I stayed because of Edvarda.
One evening she came to my hut. She had never come there before. She knocked and I called for her to come in, and she came in and looked around at my things—my guns, my books, my hunting trophies.
'You live simply,' she said.
'Yes,' I said.
She sat down on the one chair. I sat on the bed.
'I wanted to give you something before I go,' she said, and held out a feather—a bird's feather, brown and striped. 'I found it in the forest.'
I took it from her. Our fingers touched for a moment.
'Thank you,' I said.
She looked at me. There was something in her eyes—something I could not read.
'Lieutenant Glahn,' she said, 'do you like me?'
The directness of the question startled me.
'Yes,' I said. 'Very much.'
She nodded, as if she had expected this answer. Then she got up.
'Good night,' she said, and went out.
I sat holding the feather and listening to her footsteps fade into the forest.

title: Part III
paragraphs: She did not leave when she had said she would. Something delayed her, and she stayed on through the long northern autumn. The light changed. The days grew shorter and colder, and the forest changed its colors and then shed its leaves, and still Edvarda stayed.

But something had changed between us. After the evening she came to my hut, something shifted—some balance that had held now tilted. She became more distant, more cold. She began to be seen with the Doctor, a man named Müller who had arrived in the autumn—a thin, precise man with spectacles, who was everything I was not.
I watched her with the Doctor and felt something ugly move in me. I had no claim on her. I had never told her what I felt. And yet I felt it keenly—this exclusion, this replacement.

I went hunting more and more, staying in the forest for days at a time, sleeping under the trees when I had to. Aesop was my only companion. We went far into the interior, to lakes and rivers where no one else went, and I shot game and lived off what I shot, and thought about nothing but the forest and the sounds of the forest.
But always when I returned to the settlement, there was Edvarda, and there was the Doctor.
One afternoon I met her on the path to the trading post. She was alone.
'You have been away,' she said.
'Yes,' I said.
She looked at me. There were shadows under her eyes.
'I thought you might have gone south,' she said.
'No,' I said. 'I am still here.'
We walked together for a while in silence.
'The Doctor is leaving next week,' she said.
I said nothing.
'He has asked me to go with him,' she said. 'To the city. He wants to marry me.'
I stopped walking.
'And will you?' I said.
She stopped too and looked at me.
'I don't know,' she said. 'I wanted to ask you something first.'
My heart was hammering.
'Ask,' I said.
But she was silent for a long moment, looking at me with those large tilted eyes.
'Never mind,' she said at last. 'I don't know what I wanted to ask.' And she walked on.
I stood and watched her go.
That night I took the feather she had given me and held it in my hand for a long time. Then I went to sleep.
In the morning I heard that Edvarda had agreed to marry the Doctor.

I said nothing to anyone. I went into the forest with Aesop and walked all day, until I was far from the settlement and the sound of the sea was gone and there was only the wind in the pines and the sound of my own footsteps.
And I thought: the forest is real, and the wind is real, and the smell of pine is real. These things do not change. These things do not leave.
And then I thought of Edvarda and I could not make myself believe any of it.
The Doctor left with Edvarda before the first snow. I watched from the edge of the forest as they went aboard the boat. She did not look back.
I went back to my hut and sat down. Aesop put his head on my knee. The forest was very still.
I stayed on through the winter. I don't know why. There was nothing to keep me there, and yet I stayed. I hunted and I read and I sat in the evenings listening to the wind.
In the spring I went south at last. I left Aesop with Mack—the old dog was not well enough for the journey—and I went.

I have been in the city since then. I do my work, I go through my days. But sometimes in the evening, when the light is a certain way, I think of the forest and the sea and the long northern summer. I think of a girl with large tilted eyes holding out a feather.
I think of those days and I am filled with something I cannot name—something gentle and bitter all at once.
That is all.
